sentences.text: I think two days.
And then suddenly, with a jerk, somebody sent me in German, which I don't recommend, because it's too realistic-- on the video-- nuns from New Guinea-- to make me happy.
I lend it already to somebody.
I couldn't cope with it.
And this jerk of the-- this ruthless jerk of a-- it came to a stop.
And millions of words have been written about the end of the railway track in Auschwitz-- the end of the line.
That was it.
It didn't go any further.
And I saw they opened this huge bank.
They undid the cattle train.
And there was screaming-- outside scream.
That was part of the system.
You learn in two days that that is nothing-- only screaming.
But then I never heard anybody raise their voice against me-- and suddenly, the screaming.
And they made my father-- he was sitting next to the door.
They said in Yiddish-- everything in Yiddish-- which I didn't understand.
I don't speak it.
It's jargon.
By my mother, it's for-- for under people.
It's not for real people.
No-- no normal people speak Yiddish-- in my imagination.
So they started to scream.
The kapos.
And then, there was no-- no German soldier, no German SS, only screaming kapos.
And they were all in smart, striped uniforms.
[NON-ENGLISH].
That's the same thing.
Faster, faster.
Everything in Auschwitz, everything you had to be faster.
You had to run, run, run, for nothing.
That was pure sadism-- elimination of the-- of the weak.
And you learn to live with it.
After two days, you don't hear.
You don't even hear what they say-- not so interesting.
And we-- my-- said to my father, jump.
He said in his nice German-- sitting down-- I can't get up.
I'm sorry.
There is my wooden leg hanging over there.
And they said, that stays in the train for the German Wehrmacht-- the sons of the fathers is whom he fought for Kaiser and fatherland.
And they put out a blanket and made him jump into the blanket.
And they shot him.
So that was the end of Paul Haas in my life.
Yeah.
So I realized that this is a major crime, and I thought-- I knew that there was in the Hague a Supreme Court-- that I knew.
And I made a little-- in my memory-- and I thought-- but I didn't budge.
I didn't cry.
I didn't-- my mother-- my mother was sent to the site with women and her two children.
And they was in their smart leather coat, and then with a big, huge torch.
And he shone into our faces for a about-- dismal fraction of a second.
And that was night.
Everything in Auschwitz happened at night.
During the day, we slept.
And with that, he decided right, left, right, left, right-- for the-- how could he judge?
We all-- everybody was strained.
Everybody-- I was a strong girl.
So he sent me to the right, and my mother and my little sister, who was 13, to the left.
I was there amongst all these strange, hysterical women, hiding their babies, screaming their head off, completely out of their mind.
I thought, no.
This is not for me.
I go with my mother, wherever she goes.
That was again to-- that my needs of protection, I guess.
And I went away and marched myself to the left side.
And I called, [NON-ENGLISH] to my sister.
And I-- we had sailor-- matching sailor coats with golden buttons.
And I held the golden button and pulled her towards me.
And I called [NON-ENGLISH] three times.
And psychiatrists of the universe are trying to break that pain I have in me that my mother didn't turn around, because she could have lived.
I would have pulled her over.
I blame myself that I wasn't quick enough to pull her over with me.
I held my sister by her button when Mengele run after me-- pooped me under my scarf and pulled me back, and said to me-- in very good German-- I send you before over there.
There is a reason to it.
Yes.
And in my history of psychiatric wards all over the world, every night, sister was told never to shone a torch at me, because I go off my brain.
I go quite hysterical and very nasty on many occasions.
And so I was there with all these women.
And then came the drill, which you know-- that strip naked.
Me with all my panties and my bras and all the-- jazz, which I took.
I-- we had to get out of it.
In a big hall-- big, big hall.
It was called Canada.
I don't know for what reason.
And there was a woman standing next to me.
And they told her, hurry up.
[NON-ENGLISH], everything, [NON-ENGLISH].
Get dressed.
She said, I am Frau Irriza-- Irriza.
My husband is a rabbi from Galina.
He has never seen me naked, and you will not see me naked either.
So he hit her so hard.
She stripped faster than I.
And she survived with us, with me.
And I visited her in Israel just before she died.
And she had a daughter, Stella, who was with her.
And her husband, the rabbi, and their son, Benny, went with the man.
I met Manny-- Benny in Israel, who I'm sure, if they talk in Israel, will speak in a video, who is a chef on Israeli ships.
And he survived by teaching the SS how to play false card games.
As a-- he's younger than me.
So he was 15.
Men?
Well, the first--
Good.
When you ask men, I didn't see any men until the second night.
The drill was like this.
First, you went under the terrible shower with your shaven head-- a hot, hot boiling shower on the naked head, then out in the snow.
It was snowing in October in Poland-- to dry.
There were no towels by-- supplied by the SS-- then in.
Then you got your bread.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH] the-- or [NON-ENGLISH] they called it in Polish.
And you got this thing.
And you got a bit of artificial honey or artificial sausage.
Everything was artificial.
The bread was artificial.
And then they led you to the latrine.
A latrine is a long row of holes in the ground, one against the other.
That's what latrines are.
They were full of muck and shit and vomit.
And I thought, I am not sitting in this dreck when my mother taught me, you never sit on other people's toilet.
And I couched.
And they caught me from the back with a stick.
And I had a terrible pain.
And I fell into the latrine.
And I held my breath.
And somebody promptly stole it.
So I was full of shit, no paper, no underwear, stark naked.
I sat there, and I thought, I can handle everything.
They can shout.
They can scream.
They can shave me.
I had a coat, a coat over nothing.
But this life-- and when people ask me, what was my worst experience?
I will tell them that I couldn't clean my teeth and that there was no toilet paper.
Because that is-- if they deprive you of that, they might as well take you.
And suddenly, I saw striped trousers walking towards me and big wooden clogs.
And it was a man.
And I looked for the star of David, and he had a big pink triangle and the number.
And he carried a bucket.
And he carried a brush.
And he said in a very beautiful German-- not in Yiddish-- hurry, hurry.
Get up.
I have work to do.
I said, I can't.
I am so ashamed.
What is this?
And I looked at this pink triangle-- to make conversation, to get him to go away.
I couldn't get up naked like that.
And he said to me, I'm gay.
[NON-ENGLISH].
What did I know what [NON-ENGLISH] was?
I said, is that a religion?
He said, no.
I'm a homosexual.
So I still didn't know.
And he explained to me that with his partner, he lived in Munich where he was an academic portrait painter.
And the-- the Gestapo under paragraph 175, which was for perversion, broke into the flat.
The partner went to Dachau.
He came to Auschwitz in 1940, four years before I met him-- and a German, a non-Jew.
And because he was not a Jew, they asked him sarcastically, when they shaved him and put on the pajama, what is your profession?
He said, I'm a academic portrait painter from Munich.
We have just the right thing for you.
And they took him to a laundry and gave him a bucket with whitewash and a brush.
And since 1940, seven days a week, 10 hours a day, he had to brush the shit off the latrine so that he shouldn't come out of experience with his brush.
And that's what Germans did to Germans.
He came-- he asked me, where is your bunk?
That was a hard question.
We all looked alike.
Women without hair and with the expressionless faces, like milk-- no-- like water soup-- like water soup.
Everybody looks the same.
Take a hair away from a woman and her smile away, and everything.
So I found my sister.
We are crouching-- a little girl.
She was soft.
She was petrified.
And he said, I will be back.
Don't worry.
I come back later.
And he left.
So when you ask me, did you see a man?
Yes, I saw a man.
He came back with a little [NON-ENGLISH] and two hot jacket potatoes.
And during my stay in Auschwitz, that strange man came to these two little girls from a strange country he didn't even know and who were Jewish, and he brought them two jacket potatoes.
And may his memory be blessed, because he contributed to my survival.
That is a fact.
One day, at the end of October-- I knew always the date.
You memorize these things.
He came running very upset.
And said, Kitty, I don't think I will see you again, because I have to go.
They are clearing out now the camps, and I have to go to Oranienburg-- that's near Berlin-- to dismantle the concentration camp.
But I give you good advice.
Tomorrow is going to be a great [NON-ENGLISH]-- the selection.
I said, right, left again?
He said, no, but there are some executives here from a big weaving plant.
And they need 50 weavers to take back to Upper Silesia.
So I said, what?
What has that to do with me?
I can't weave.
He said, you will tell them.
When they ask you what you are doing, you tell them you are a weaver.
I said, they shoot me.
They said, they won't.
But they will shoot you if you stay here.
Please.
Get yourself out of here, because I can't help you anymore.
I said, what chance have I got?
We were 1,000 women in one barrack, and they needed 50?
So he said, you speak a beautiful German.
Talk to them like you talk to me.
Kitty, do you think that's why he noticed or helped you, because of the way you talked to him?
Why did he help you?
Because I was so refreshingly different.
I didn't speak Yiddish.
I didn't scream about my mother and my father.
And because I was so young!
He felt sorry for me!
He knew what can happen to me.
I didn't.
And so next day, stark naked, parading, end of October-- it was so cold.
By the hand, my sister-- and I am not tattooed.
Because I was in a transit camp from Birkenau straight into Auschwitz, where people went for labor.
They're selected for labor.
In Birkenau it was death.
So I had a number around my neck-- and 27,258, and my sister had 27,259.
And there was a list.
And that is my challenge to Mr.
Spielberg-- may he listen to me-- that there were many lists floating around like the one of Mr.
Schindler, with the same noble motive-- to get cheap labor in Auschwitz, out of Auschwitz.
I am one of them, and I didn't make the movie.
And he had a list there and no names, like on Schindler's-- only the numbers.
And I came.
He said to me, no, young girl, [NON-ENGLISH]-- little one.
What can you do?
I said, I am a weaver.
He said, you are a little bit too young to be a weaver.
And where did you weave?
I said, my parents had a weaving plant in Czechoslovakia in the Sudetenland.
He said, that's very famous.
Tell me.
Is this one here your sister?
My little sister-- I said, yes.
[NON-ENGLISH]-- write it down.
And they looked, picked up my number, and copied it out.
And I got on the list.
And that's how I got out of Auschwitz.
No.
I think he was a foreman, a factory foreman.
He wore overalls, parting on the side, like everybody-- [NON-ENGLISH].
No.
You were lingering around there, waiting to go somewhere-- do something.
There was no guessing anymore.
The man with the pink triangle-- isn't that a lovely memory?
And when they shaved us-- how I pissed on him-- lovely memory.
And when we left Auschwitz, they told us we are going to this weaving plant.
I made it.
So you saw wherever you looked in Auschwitz the barbed wire-- you know, with the electric currents of wire.
And you looked, and they tried-- the train moved.
And we started to go, and to go, and to go.
And there was still the barbed wire.
I didn't believe them one word.
I said, that's all swindle.
We're going to another gas chamber.
And suddenly, on the 28th of October, 1944, I saw the sky-- a murky, gray sky-- no barbed wire.
People were embracing each other.
We all were waiting for the moment.
We understood one thing.
If we stay healthy, there is no gas chambers anymore where we are going.
And then--
By the grapevine.
You heard all these things.
People told you.
People had friends.
There were people who had kapo friends who fed them, heard they had kapo friends who pulled them out of line and sent them to good camps in Berlin or somewhere else.
People had friends.
And the Polish under work-- underground worked very well.
They were all from the large ghetto, from the Litzmannstadt.
It was called Litzmannstadt.
And when that was liquidated, they sent them to Auschwitz.
And they got into senior positions.
It was a Polish mafia.
And one day, another woman wanted to hit me, but only she wanted I hold her.
And she said to me something which I didn't understand-- in Yiddish.
And I said to her, [NON-ENGLISH].
And she--
I don't speak jargon.
And she looked at me, and she said, [NON-ENGLISH].
That is my first Yiddish sentence I said at every occasion.
She doesn't speak-- [NON-ENGLISH] is Yiddish-- mother tongue.
She doesn't speak her mother's tongue.
And every time they spoke to me, I was doing passive resistance.
I said, I don't speak [NON-ENGLISH].
When you come to Israel, and you ask a bastard to give you a seat in the bus, he will tell you in English.
He will tell you, [NON-ENGLISH] only Hebrew.
So there he must have learned that in Auschwitz-- [NON-ENGLISH].
Good.
The women I avoided, because the grown-up-- felt lost-- I lost interest in the grown-up world.
And I promised myself if the grown-up world is so rotten as the grown-ups whom I met now during my quick growing up, I don't wish to grow up ever again.
And I succeeded.
I-- I'm not a grown-up person-- psychologically.
I never will be, and I don't want to be.

And-- but there were girls, Johanna Orenstein, who was baptized, who was Roman Catholic in a convent like myself, but in a different town, and who prayed all the time the-- [NON-ENGLISH], always in Latin, prating all day long.
It didn't help her, because she got typhoid fever.
And she was completely emaciated in Merzdorf, in that camp where I went weaving as a famous weaver.
So, Mr.
Winkler, the headmaster, the principal, the director, the boss-- but they hung him after the war.
He did run fast enough-- put me to two rooms-- huge like this couch-- rooms, and said, go.
I saw the little things, little balls jumping up and down.
I saw the-- [LAUGHS] --threads tearing up and down.
But I just let it happen.
[LAUGHS]
And there was this bitch, Itka.
She had a boyfriend, a Greek prisoner of war.
There were a lot of non-Jewish prisoners of war.
When you ask about men, I never managed another boyfriend after the pink triangle.
But she had one.
She had shoes.
She had socks.
She had a turban.
She had food.
And she was there by herself also on two rooms.
But she was there long before me.
And she let me go into the sauce.
Winkler came, stopped the machine, wanted to hit me.
And I put up my finger, and I said to him, [NON-ENGLISH]!
Don't you dare.
And he dropped his hand.
He wanted to hit me.
We-- and he said to me in a very Silesian, miserable German dialect, that I have purposely damaged the property of the Reich.
I said, I did not do such a thing.
I did not know it is property of the Reich.
I-- we had different rules where I come from.
And you haven't shown me-- it's your fault.
You should have told me.
It's your fault.
And he swallowed it.
He realized that he made a mistake.
And he fixed it.
He showed me how to fix it-- if I break a thread.
And I did break many threads, because I didn't think it was important.
And I thought it was such a joke.
We were weaving parachute linen for the German Luftwaffe.
In March, in April, in May, 1945, I, when the world was in pieces, I was standing, weaving for the German Luftwaffe.
And one day, in the-- the routine in the camp, in that surface-- always very simple.
[NON-ENGLISH] in the morning-- get up.
We jumped.
We had beautiful showers there.
We showered.
We put on our clothes.
We didn't have lice.
We were OK.
We were-- it was hygienic.
We had to clean every Sunday.
I never did, but others did.
And then-- then we got our coffee for breakfast, I think, or whatever-- or soup, maybe.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH].
And then we went-- but then they counted us.
They counted us three times a day in the freezing-- it was so cold.
So my feet were all swollen from the cold.
And then came Christmas, 1944-- Christmas Eve.
You must understand that there were girls with me, women, a bit older than me, who never knew they were Jewish.

Because they were German, and they suddenly discovered that one Aryan-- non-Aryan grandmother, when they had to prove their Aryan descendancy, [NON-ENGLISH], and they couldn't prove their Aryan [NON-ENGLISH].
And they were sent to Theresienstadt.
And in the end too, there was a girl, a [NON-ENGLISH].
She was a film producer or director, film director for UFA, for the greatest German film works.
Her husband was a-- a non-Jewish German.
And he divorced her when he found out that she had missing-- that one Jewish grandmother.
I said, I hope you will-- she said to me, hit him?
He did it for the Fuhrer.
Search me what they saw in this Fuhrer.
I don't know.
But I only know that on Christmas Eve, these non-Jewish girls went and said to the commander Fuhrer whom never saw, that was-- that came around only by women.
Please, can we go to midnight mass?
We are Christians.
We believe in Jesus' birth.
And we wish to go to midnight mass.
So she said, go back to your barrack, and I will give you a Christmas you will not forget in a hurry.
And she let us to stand up outside when it was dark-- not a star in the sky, because it was snowing.
And so slowly came the snow down.
And on the balcony upstairs, you heard the laughter.
They had a party.
You heard the corks of the champagne.
And we were standing down there.
And as silently as the snowflakes fell down, one after the other fainted and fell into the snow.
I closed my eyes like this, and I waited.
And I prayed, please, God, [NON-ENGLISH], God.
Let me faint.
My sister was the first one.
I want to go out of this freezing.
And then I started to look around when I saw I can't faint.
So I might as well wait what is going to happen.
They won't leave us there forever.
And I looked around, and I saw the Germans had the windows lit with one bulb.
And they had this Christmas tree-- little, little, mini, mini-- with one miserable half candle, maybe lit from last year, with one shriveled apple and full of photos with [NON-ENGLISH] bands on them.
So I thought to myself, the bastards.
They have it tough.
They have it tough.
They have a war too.
But they have one only thing ahead of us.
They are together.
They are kept together.
They in this together, and that makes it easier for them.
And I was their last three.
Mrs.
Schtaudt, maybe you will get to her.
I will ask her.
She was there with me.
She's here in Sydney-- and another girl, who is in Israel, we three survived the fainting business.
And everybody else was taken away.
And then as a accolade, the German SS Sturmbannfuhrer lady threw down the peelings of her shriveled apples, which they ate inside.
And I so remember, I collected as many as I could carry with the snow in my hand and the peels of these shriveled, miserable apples, to go inside and look for my sister.
Because I-- I wanted to share with her the joy.
And we had central heating there, believe it or not.
And we-- the girls told me what to do with it.
I ate it as it was.
I was so hungry.
But they had the patience to put it on the central heating and-- and had a cooked apple, baked apple.
That was our Christmas Eve, 1944.
And then it went very rapidly towards the end.
We saw nothing of it.
Nobody would have known there is the end of the war, until one morning, which was the 8th of May, 1945.
Nobody yelled [NON-ENGLISH].
So we didn't move.
Somebody knocked at the door.
We didn't move.
A man's voice said-- croaky, old man's voice-- please.
Try and get up.
So-- [LAUGHS] --when you are subjected to [NON-ENGLISH], are you going to answer the call of a nice, gentle old man?
Get stuffed.
[LAUGHS]
We turned around.
And-- [LAUGHS] --we thought, what is this?
And he said, the SS has left.
They took the kapos with them.
That is the infamy of the Polish kapos.
They all went with the henchmen together.
They took them with them for the services done to them.
And they took them.
And we were alone.
Leave the gentleman a--
